title: GPU inference bottleneck limits coverage matrix scaling to 1-2 concurrent submissions
reasoning: Ada's capacity model shows coverage matrix generation consumes 180-200s of semantic inference time per 100 MB evidence pack (NFR-LAT-003). This is a GPU-serialized task: single GPU instance processes one inference job at a time. At 5x growth (5 concurrent submissions), the queue would accumulate to 900s peak latency (p95 600s SLA violated). GPU batch efficiency requires 2-3 concurrent inferences per GPU; this is only achievable if submissions aggregate or if system accepts queue backlog. Current hardware: 1 GPU. Required by 2028: 5 GPU instances (5 concurrent submissions) or adopt hybrid approach (pre-filter evidence to 50-100 MB packs, reduce inference time to 120-240s).
recommendation: Procure 4-5 GPU instances (Jetson Orin or A100) by end of 2026 to stay ahead of growth curve. Alternatively, optimize OCR pipeline (use fast-OCR library + batched inference) to reduce semantic classification time from 200s to 100s, halving GPU requirements. Monitor queue depth weekly; auto-scale policy should trigger at queue depth >5 items.

title: AI narrative service GPU memory ceiling at 10 concurrent narratives (2028 demand)
reasoning: Noyce's latency budget allocates 900s for AI inference per narrative (NFR-LAT-007, 30 min SLA). At 20 concurrent narrative requests (5x growth), system requires 10 GPU instances (1800s / 900s inference per GPU = 2 concurrent narratives per GPU). Each 40GB A100 GPU serves 2 concurrent narratives at 100 tokens/sec streaming rate. GPU memory bottleneck: 13B LLM model = 26 GB; 2 concurrent inferences = 52 GB RAM (exceeds 40 GB). Mitigation: smaller model (7B = 14 GB; fits 2 concurrent), quantization (4-bit = 7 GB per 7B model), or vLLM paged attention (reduces memory by 30%). Current deployment: 0 instances (feature not yet live). By 2028, vLLM or equivalent is mandatory.
recommendation: Deploy vLLM inference engine (paged attention reduces memory overhead 30-40%). Test 7B model quantization (4-bit) for quality/latency trade-off. Procure 10 A100 GPU instances by end 2027 to handle 20 concurrent narratives at 1800s p95 SLA. Fallback: if GPU cost prohibitive, implement synchronous narrative batch processing (reduce concurrency to 3-5, accept longer queue latency 30-45 min).

title: SECRET/STRAP tier rebuild latency catastrophic failure (2-4 hour outage on incident)
reasoning: Ada's capacity model identifies SECRET tier as air-gapped, on-premises, single-instance (no failover). Rebuild time on failure: 2-4 hours (JSP 440 compliance, secure facility re-provisioning). By 2028, SECRET tier stores 3-5 GB of MOD-classified GCAP/Tempest autonomy assurance evidence. Single-instance failure = total loss of access during rebuild; regulatory non-compliance if incident investigation requires classified evidence. OFFICIAL tier: 4-replica cloud setup (resilient). OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE: 2 on-prem replicas (failover available). SECRET: 1 instance, no failover = unacceptable risk.
recommendation: Implement hot-standby SECRET tier replica (air-gapped, same facility, manual failover). Reduce rebuild time to <30 minutes via pre-staged VM image + configuration snapshot. Document incident response procedure (crew notification, investigation hold time, evidence retrieval path). Alternatively, negotiate with MOD for cloud-hosted SECRET tier (AWS GovCloud or equivalent), enabling auto-failover (reduces rebuild to <5 minutes).

title: Panel action-log write contention at p95 500 ms SLA will break under 10+ concurrent panelists
reasoning: Noyce's latency budget (NFR-LAT-009) allocates 150 ms for immutable-log write + 100 ms replication quorum. At 10 concurrent panelists during voting, action-log write rate peaks at 30-50 actions/sec (3-5 actions per panelist per second during rapid polling). Distributed ledger quorum write (2-of-3 replicas ACK) exhibits tight coupling: if any replica is slow (network jitter, fsync latency), entire quorum stalls. Testing shows single-instance latency baseline 150 ms; under 10-way contention, lock-wait overhead + fsync jitter can push p95 to 600-800 ms (violating 500 ms SLA by 20-60%). Current panel size: 8-12 members; peak concurrency ~8 (not all writing simultaneously). By 2028, larger panels (15+ members, longer deliberations) may trigger contention.
recommendation: Implement optimistic locking for panel action-log writes (version-based conflicts; retry on collision). Separate action-log into per-panelist write path (no contention) with async aggregation to master log (trades 1-5 sec consistency delay for contention-free writes). Alternatively, upgrade replication to quorum-gossip (3-way replication with early-abort if 2 replicas ACK, no wait for 3rd) to reduce consensus latency from 150 ms to 80-100 ms.

title: WAN failover breaks audit-log write SLA (500 ms p95); air-gapping SECRET tier replication costly
reasoning: Audit-log distributed ledger requires 3-replica quorum write (2-of-3 ACK). LAN latency (same AZ, <5 ms round-trip) allows 150 ms quorum consensus (Noyce budget). WAN failover (cross-region replica for disaster recovery) introduces 30-100 ms latency per network hop. In WAN mode, 500 ms p95 SLA becomes unachievable (3-way quorum = 300 ms baseline + jitter). SECRET tier air-gap (on-prem facility) makes WAN failover infeasible (dedicated air-gapped replicas required; cost prohibitive for third replica). OFFICIAL tier: can use cloud cross-region (AZ failover sufficient). OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE: on-prem 2 replicas (no WAN).
recommendation: For OFFICIAL tier: implement AZ failover (2 replicas in AZ-1, 1 in AZ-2, both same region <20 ms latency). Audit-log SLA remains <500 ms. For SECRET tier: establish hot-standby replica in same facility (air-gapped); eliminate WAN failover. Disaster recovery (multi-site backup): async archive to S3 with 1-2 hour RPO (acceptable for audit trail). Accept that SECRET tier has 2-4 hour RTO on total facility loss; mitigation = strict physical security + redundant power/cooling.

title: Swarm consensus protocol O(n²) latency breaks DAA encounter resolution SLA at 10+ vehicles
component: DAA sensor data fusion and encounter resolution
reasoning: DAA encounter resolution requires pairwise de-confliction consensus: each aircraft compares trajectory with all swarmmates (n-1 comparisons per aircraft). 5-vehicle swarm: 10 pairwise comparisons, ~50 ms consensus time (within 500 ms latency budget per NFR-LAT-012). 10-vehicle swarm: 45 pairwise comparisons, ~200 ms (tight but achievable). 20-vehicle swarm: 190 pairwise comparisons, ~800 ms (violates 500 ms SLA; unsafe). Real-time coordination: broadcast-gossip at 1-5 Hz; pairwise checks must complete within ~200 ms (inter-aircraft message interval). Current deployment: 5-vehicle Protector RG Mk1 (safe). Project Alvina: 7 vehicles (safe margin). GCAP/Tempest: 10-20 vehicles (SAF RISK).
recommendation: Implement hierarchical swarm clustering (sub-swarms of 3-4 aircraft de-conflict internally, parent layer de-conflicts clusters). Reduces pairwise checks from O(n²) to O(4 + k) where k = number of clusters. Example: 10 vehicles in 3 clusters (4+4+2) = 16 internal checks + 3 inter-cluster checks = 19 comparisons (vs. 45); latency reduced 50%. Requires coordination protocol redesign; test in simulation by end of 2026. For GCAP/Tempest (10+ vehicles), hierarchical coordination is mandatory.

title: C2 link bandwidth shared-spectrum contention at 3+ concurrent swarms
reasoning: C2 link budget: 100-500 kbps per vehicle (Noyce NFR-LAT-013 SLA <1s round-trip). Single swarm (5 vehicles): 500-2500 kbps total (< 3 Mbps LTE per-device capacity; no issue). Three concurrent swarms (15 vehicles): 1500-7500 kbps (7.5 Mbps total). LTE network cell capacity varies; urban cells 10-50 Mbps shared across users. Three concurrent swarms at same test site may exceed fair-share if other traffic present. Shared spectrum (unlicensed 2.4 GHz ISM band used by many trials) risks interference. Mitigation: ensure each swarm has dedicated cellular data plan (separate LTE APN) or licensed RF spectrum allocation per trial.
recommendation: Negotiate dedicated LTE APNs with operator for each authorized trial (ensures fair-share QoS). For remote trials without LTE, fall back to satellite C2 (400 kbps max; reduces real-time telemetry to 1 Hz, acceptable for non-DAA encounters). By 2028, if 3+ concurrent swarm trials planned, reserve licensed RF spectrum band (CAA airspace regulator can coordinate). Cost: GBP 5-10K per trial for spectrum licensing.

title: Applicant intake traffic spikes during quarterly panel season will require pre-scaling +2 web instances
reasoning: Ada's capacity model shows peak concurrent applicants = 25 (peak intake rate 2-3 applications/week during trial season). Web tier throughput: ~500 req/s per instance (measured). At 25 concurrent applicants with 20 req/sec per applicant (submission workflow = workspace create + upload + readiness check = 10-20 sequential requests), expect ~500 req/s sustained. Single instance breaks at 500-800 req/s. By 2028, peak intake rate = 8-10 applications/week (Project Alvina, GCAP/Tempest cohorts), peak concurrent = 50 applicants (expect ~1000 req/s). Current capacity: 2 instances. Required by 2028: 3-4 instances baseline + 1 auto-scale buffer.
recommendation: Implement scheduled auto-scaling: 2 weeks before each quarterly panel (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4), increase web tier from 2 to 4 instances. CDN edge cache (CloudFront) should front the BFF to absorb spike in static asset requests (reduces origin load 30-40%). Monitor request rate per applicant; if average >30 req/applicant (indicating rework/re-upload cycles), alert case officers to prioritize readiness coaching.

title: Clause library semantic search adds 5-10s latency; vector embedding model serving must be async
reasoning: Ada's capacity model estimates clause library at 200-500 MB by 2028. Full-text search (Elasticsearch): <500 ms p95 (interactive). Semantic search (vector embedding + cosine similarity): requires LLM embedding inference (separate GPU worker). Current design: not specified in requirements (gap in NFR-LAT-017 which specifies 5s search SLA but doesn't distinguish full-text vs. semantic). If semantic search invoked synchronously per-query, 5-10s embedding latency exceeds 5s total SLA. Mitigation: pre-compute embeddings on library update (quarterly); serve from vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector in PostgreSQL). At 500 MB library × 4 versions retained × 5-year retention = 10 GB cumulative embeddings (~80 GB in vector space).
recommendation: Deploy async embedding pre-computation: on library revision (3-4x/year), trigger batch embedding job (5-30 min compute time per library version). Store embeddings in pgvector plugin (PostgreSQL) or separate vector DB (Pinecone; cost ~GBP 100/month). At query time, hybrid search: full-text index (Elasticsearch) + vector search (pgvector) executed in parallel; return merged results in <500 ms p95. Fallback to full-text only if vector search stalls.

title: Evidence store metadata query index must be separate from object store to avoid O(n) scan at scale
reasoning: Ada's capacity model projects 140 GB cumulative evidence by 2028 (1000+ objects if average 100 MB per case). Evidence-discovery queries (NFR-LAT-017: 'find all evidence from aircraft X, 2026-2027, OFFICIAL tier') require metadata search. S3 ListObjects is O(n) scan (slow for >1000 objects). Without indexed metadata, archive search SLA (5s p95) becomes unachievable at scale. Current architecture: not specified. Gap: evidence metadata (case_id, trial_date, classification_tier, aircraft_id, keywords) must be indexed in separate DB (not S3 object metadata alone).
recommendation: Implement metadata index (PostgreSQL or Elasticsearch): track every evidence object with (s3_key, case_id, trial_date, tier, aircraft_id, keywords). Index size: ~1 KB per object × 1000 objects = 1 MB (negligible). Query: 'aircraft A1 + 2026-2027 + OFFICIAL' → index lookup <100 ms; S3 GetObject for results <1s. Update metadata on every evidence upload (add to index before S3 PutObject completes). Cost: <GBP 100/month for indexed metadata DB.

title: Onboard compute TDP budget (10-20 W) limits autonomy complexity; advanced trials require Jetson Orin upgrade
reasoning: Ada's capacity model shows onboard compute TDP budget 10-20 W per vehicle (battery endurance trade-off: 1 W = 10-20 min flight time lost). Protector RG Mk1 (basic autonomy): 40-50% CPU utilization = 5-10 W (safe margin). Project Alvina (swarm coordination): 60-70% CPU = 10-15 W (tight). GCAP/Tempest (advanced ML inference + formation control): 90%+ CPU = 18-20 W (thermal throttling risk). Current hardware: Jetson Nano or TX2 (8-15 W TDP). At 85% utilization, Nano throttles. Jetson Orin (25 W TDP) can sustain 85% utilization without throttling, but exceeds battery endurance budget (reduces flight time 20-30%).
recommendation: For GCAP/Tempest trials (2027-2028), mandate Jetson Orin (or equivalent 15-20 W ARM compute) + power budget review (reduce flight duration from 90 min to 60-75 min). Alternatively, off-load non-critical ML inference to ground (post-flight analysis only), keeping onboard compute at <70% utilization. Test battery thermal curves by Q4 2026 to quantify flight-time penalty of advanced autonomy (should be <10% reduction for acceptable trials).
